extracts.extract: We consider data to be any computerized file that uses columns and rows (a tabular structure) to organize data that are represented as text, numbers, dates and the like. These files can be manipulated using programs like spreadsheets and database managers, and can be transmitted over computer networks.

We usually prefer to get raw data. Raw data afford us more flexibility when it comes to analysis. We can make our own decisions about how to summarize. Raw data make it easier for us to examine our data and test the integrity—or quality—of our data.

Working effectively with data requires more than computing. It requires thought and reflection on our own parts. As we climb the pyramid, we rely less on computers and more on our own experiences and thoughts.

One of the big challenges in becoming data literate is being able to quickly identify data and determine which government agency keeps them. If the data are posted on the Internet, we can find and download them. If data are not posted online, we request them from the agency. To meet that challenge we need to develop a data state of mind, one that opens our eyes to the proliferation of data online, in government agency mainframes and servers, and in university libraries.

Agencies collect, analyze and publish data for one or more of three primary reasons: (1) they are required to do so by law, (2) agencies believe the data will help them execute their missions or (3) agencies are participating in an open-government effort.
extracts.chapter: How and Why Agencies Collect, Analyze and Publish Data

extracts.extract: Our goals for working with the data can differ greatly from the goals of the people at the agencies that collect them. For instance, we may be interested in determining which industry group's employees contributed the most money to candidates, but our state probably records the occupation and employer—not industry category—of each contributor. That means we won't be able to answer that question without a lot of additional research and changes to our data.
extracts.chapter: How and Why Agencies Collect, Analyze and Publish Data

extracts.extract: If you're interested in a database, get a copy of the form that's used to feed data into it. Forms tell a lot about what you can expect to find in databases. You should assume that all of the data collected on the form will be entered. You may be incorrect, but you should start with the assumption that more data are available.

Web form URLs with a .php extension are a clue to the presence of a database. PHP is a programming language that lets Web forms pass data to databases. Also be on the lookout for .cfm (Adobe ColdFusion) and .asp (Microsoft Active Server Pages), which can also pass data from a Web form to a database.

Use Google Advanced Search to find data files. The trick is to think of words that appear on webpages that link to downloadable data. Try 'downloadable data site:.gov' to look for pages with those terms on government websites. You could narrow further by adding 'filetype:xls' for Excel files.

Before we download any data, we need to practice safe computing and document our work. Create a word processing document or file with the name Data_Notebook; this is going to be where we take notes about the data we get and what we do with them. Enter the date, then the dataset name and URL. Don't forget to include details about the data, such as the number of rows, time span, update frequency, and source.

When downloading data from a government website, make sure you also download a copy of any documentation. The data documentation, often stored as a Word, PDF or other document file, usually is essential in helping understand what's in the data file. There's no standard name for the documentation, so it might be called record layout, file layout, data dictionary or something entirely different.

The documentation usually provides some key pieces of information about the data set: table names along with record counts for each table; column or field names in each table along with a field description, type of data (character, number, date, etc.) and width; and codes and their meanings.

Before using data from a nongovernmental source, ask: How did the NGO obtain the data? What did the NGO do to process or change the data? What interest does the NGO have in making the data available? Can you get the data directly from the official government source instead?

Government data (at least in the United States) are considered to be free of licensing rules that restrict distribution and use. For journalists, official documents and data provide an additional benefit: protection against libel suits. The fair report privilege provides a shield to journalists who base their news accounts on fair and accurate use of official sources.

Records retention schedules, audit reports, federal agency major information system listings and nongovernmental organization sites are tools particularly well suited for helping identify offline data. Records retention schedules provide details about records held by state and local government agencies.

GAO audit reports can easily run dozens of pages, but you can get to the meat quickly by reading the Summary of Findings or What GAO Found section, as well as the Scope and Methodology. If a GAO auditor reviewed databases for the report, he or she will list those in the Scope and Methodology.

Once you've identified an offline database, the agency employees who create or maintain the data can be helpful because they are the ones who are most familiar with the system. As a negotiating tactic, you can always ask to speak to the data specialist directly, in the interests of making things easier for everyone.

A good open-records request letter is clear, concise and includes enough detail about what you are seeking: the name of the database as known inside the agency; the names of the data columns if known; the time frame of the data; the format in which you'd like to receive the data; request for the documentation; request for written explanation in case of denial; fee waiver request or limitation.

Ron Nixon estimated that he files two or three FOIA requests a month, trying to obtain data for stories on his to-do list. Getting data pursuant to a FOIA request can take a while, so Nixon says he tries to give himself plenty of time. When he requests data, Nixon says he tries to be as specific as possible; he avoids making requests for everything in the database because FOIA officers in agencies usually kick back those requests.

Nixon recommends that requesters do a lot of leg work before filing requests. Sometimes he bases his requests on the information collected on federal forms. Sometimes he looks at online federal agency FOIA logs to see what data others have already requested. If someone has already requested and received data, the agency should be able to easily provide a copy.

When government agencies tell you data are private, ask them to cite the law in writing so you have a copy of the agency's legal reasoning on the record. If some of the data truly are private, you can probably get the public portion, because under FOIA and state open-records laws, agencies are supposed to disclose what's public.

When we get a data file—by downloading it or through a public records request—it's tempting to want to start analyzing it right away. But at this point, we need to pause and take time to understand our data better. What are the data's strengths? What are their weaknesses? Is anything missing? How important are those missing data? Can we use the data to accurately answer any questions that we might have?

As analysts, we have a duty to know our data inside and out. We need to have an intimate understanding of their shortcomings. We owe the consumers of our data analysis—whether that's other researchers, business clients or the public—the most accurate reading of our data that we can possibly deliver.

Blindly trusting that government agencies and others will collect and report data accurately is a neglect of duty on our part. Veteran data users will tell you that government data can run the gamut from slightly messy to so flawed that the data are unusable. Government agencies often have poor data quality control, if they have any at all.

All data are dirty. Dirty data thrive where there are poor controls during the data entry process—the old GIGO, garbage in, garbage out, effect. Data-entry clerks are low paid and perform this work for hours-long stretches. It's easy to get distracted and make mistakes, and easy to enter data inconsistently.

Changing rules for collecting data can hurt your data quality, or at least limit your analysis. Federal government agencies often tweak or even radically change their data collection methods and rules. Any comparisons across rule changes would prove inaccurate.
extracts.notes: Examples: BLS mass layoffs scope change 2004, bankruptcy law 2005, MEPS survey hold 2007

extracts.extract: FAPRI analysts look over the data in tables and create charts that help them spot data that are inconsistent or improbable. Analysts examine supply and use data to see if they are in balance—making sure the sum of production and imports equals the sum of domestic use, exports and ending stocks.

Immediately after we get a file, we want to answer a few big-picture questions before moving on to more-detailed data-integrity checks: What is the file format? How many rows do we have? Are all of the columns present with proper headers?

For detailed integrity checks, generate pivot tables that list each data value and the number of times that value appears. These checks let us take a good look at what we have to work with. Check each column against the metadata to verify values match expected formats, ranges, and patterns.

When you run data integrity checks you'll need to have your data documentation at your side. Practice safe computing by making a copy of the metadata for yourself, so you always have a copy somewhere.

Look at the column headers in Row 1 and make sure they are the same as in the metadata. It's tempting to change the headers to something that's more meaningful to us. However, it's best to practice safe computing by keeping the original names. That way, anyone who works with the data can easily refer to the metadata.

Practice safe computing: We've made changes to our sheet, so let's preserve the original and create a new, working version. Save a working version of the file. After working with data for a while you will no doubt adopt a file-naming convention that fits your workflow.

Look for suspect values—outliers, or numbers that are so extreme that they might be incorrect. Suspect values pop up in columns that contain dates or numbers. For dates, check that all values fall within the expected time frame. For amounts, look at both extremes.

We can use pivot tables to combine cities and states in our results, testing location data more rigorously than checking each field alone. This allows us to spot errors like a city listed in the wrong state.

When importing text files into Excel, we should make all of the columns text to avoid any import flubs that may delete data. Excel's General format will treat zip codes with leading zeros as numbers and lop off the leading zero. We can always change the format of the columns in Excel later.
extracts.chapter: Downloading, Unzipping and Inspecting Data Files

extracts.extract: Excel and other spreadsheet programs have built-in features and functions for data cleaning: Text to Columns to parse data stored in one column into multiple columns; Remove Duplicates to remove repeated rows; and formulas to carve out and reassemble data. OpenRefine is an open-source program from Google that lets users quickly run data integrity checks, then clean flawed values.

More-advanced data users will use database managers, such as Microsoft Access or MySQL, to clean data using string functions inserted into SQL queries. Data users who are even more advanced write scripts using programming languages like Python, Ruby, PHP or Perl to clean and transform data sets, processing huge amounts of data more quickly than traditional programs.

Use Excel's TRIM function to remove extraneous leading and trailing spaces, as well as duplicate spaces inside a text entry. Spaces technically are data (one of the basic ASCII characters) and can throw off any analysis.

OpenRefine's Clustering feature uses algorithms designed to find text values that might be the same. It employs two methods—key collision and nearest neighbor—each with multiple keying functions. This allows cleaning of misspelled or inconsistent entries en masse rather than one at a time.

Text files are perhaps the most useful format of all because they can be read, processed and exported by all computers and data analysis programs. These text files come in two different flavors: fixed-width and delimited. Delimited data are easier to import than fixed-width, because computer programs can read the delimiters and automatically determine where the column breaks go.

Use a text editor such as Notepad++ (Windows) or TextWrangler (Mac) to inspect data files before importing them into a spreadsheet. Text editors can handle large files and let you see hidden characters like tabs and carriage returns that serve as delimiters.
extracts.chapter: Downloading, Unzipping and Inspecting Data Files

extracts.extract: If agencies are unable to produce Excel files, they should be able to create ASCII text (delimited or fixed-width), because that is the format common to all computers. Under FOIA, federal agencies are supposed to provide data in the format that's requested if the agency is able to do so.

Use website navigation tabs to look for words like Data, Open Data, Transparency and Statistics when searching agency sites for data. Try typing www.agencyname.gov/data in your browser. You can also try typing data instead of www in a URL, such as data.mo.gov for a state portal.

When someone creates a data file and stores columns with date data as text, it limits our ability to analyze the file. We won't be able to run date formulas, calculate elapsed time between dates, or figure out the day of the week. Another database design problem is run-on data—when the person who created the file stuffs more data than we'd like into one column.

Let's practice safe computing and make sure we downloaded all of the data: check that we downloaded all columns, then use Ctrl-End to navigate to the bottom right corner of the spreadsheet to verify the row count matches what the source indicated.
